briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon, everyone. We will now begin the climate briefing for April 24, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Threats of Climate Change to South Korea's Food Security
sections.content: Today's major domestic and international news reports warn of severe threats posed by climate change to South Korea's food security. Analyses suggest that climate change is destabilizing the food economy, which could lead to economic ripple effects and geopolitical instability [6, 7, 8]. It is particularly emphasized that now is the time to seek urgent, data-driven solutions as South Korea could face a food crisis in 2026 [9, 12, 14]. The Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation is also raising global awareness of food issues by posing questions about how climate change affects what we eat [48].

sections.title: 🌍 Global Climate Response and Controversies
sections.content: In the international community, various efforts to address climate change have been observed, alongside some controversies. Wildfires in Georgia and Florida clearly demonstrate that climate change has led to an increase in wildfires in the eastern region, and research has also warned that extreme weather could threaten over one-third of terrestrial animal habitats by 2085 [37, 38, 65, 77]. Meanwhile, news reports have repeatedly indicated that France excluded climate change discussions from the G7 agenda to avoid conflict with the United States, suggesting difficulties in international cooperation [43, 46, 51, 68, 78, 79, 80, 82, 83]. However, positive transition efforts are also continuing, with the world's first fossil fuel transition conference commencing in Colombia, and Chinese leadership urging strict control over fossil fuels [92, 95, 96].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Domestic Climate Change Week and Carbon Neutrality Efforts
sections.content: Domestically, a commemorative ceremony for the 'UN Climate Change Convention Climate Week' was held in Yeosu, providing an important forum for discussions on finding solutions to the climate crisis [2, 3, 4, 5]. Western Power promoted its carbon neutrality achievements during 'K-GX International Week' and 'Climate Change Week,' and various local governments, including Yeongdo-gu in Busan and Jung-gu in Incheon, encouraged citizen participation by holding lights-out events and operating Climate Change Week in observance of Earth Day [1, 10, 15, 21]. Furthermore, institutional improvement efforts are ongoing, with National Assembly member Choi Hyeok-jin, for example, proposing an amendment to the Framework Act on Carbon Neutrality and Green Growth for responding to the climate crisis [20]. While these movements demonstrate the decentralization of carbon neutrality policy implementation from central to local levels, it has also been pointed out that the related structure is still incomplete [19].

sections.title: 🌱 Expansion of Renewable Energy and Policy Challenges
sections.content: In the renewable energy sector, positive advancements have emerged alongside policy challenges. Domestically, the outline of a 100GW renewable energy roadmap, encompassing everything from reclaimed land to balcony solar panels, has been revealed, and Western Power is expanding its renewable energy business division to enhance expertise [26, 28]. Active movements in the private sector are also noteworthy, including the collaboration between Hyundai Solartech and the World Environmental Industry Education Promotion Agency, and the accelerated growth of renewable energy ETFs [31, 32]. However, difficulties in policy implementation are anticipated, as government ministries initially opposed the specification of steep reduction targets in the Carbon Neutrality Act, and concerns have been raised about excessive government influence in carbon neutrality policy decisions [16, 18]. Furthermore, news of successive bankruptcies among renewable energy construction companies underscores the need for institutional support to ensure the stable growth of the renewable energy industry [30].

sections.title: ⚠️ Ecosystems in Crisis and Efforts to Protect Biodiversity
sections.content: Ecosystem destruction due to climate change is emerging as one of the most urgent issues today. The Antarctic ice kingdom is collapsing, putting penguins at risk of survival. Particularly, ahead of 'World Penguin Day,' the accelerated Antarctic spring due to abnormal weather is reportedly exacerbating starvation among penguin chicks [11, 13]. Marine heatwaves are spreading along the coasts of California and Mexico, and habitats for salt marshes and aquatic ferns are shrinking due to climate change, indicating that biodiversity is already severely impacted [49, 50, 52, 67]. In response, the African Development Bank has approved a $25 million grant for climate change response, and efforts to protect ecosystems are continuing in various regions, such as divers using underwater speakers to aid dying coral reefs [85, 93].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing has simultaneously showcased the diverse threats posed by climate change and the domestic and international efforts to overcome them. Continued attention and concrete action are required. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.title: 영국 당국, AI 데이터센터의 탄소 배출량 영향 크게 과소평가
articles.original_title: Officials hugely underestimated impact of AI datacentres on UK carbon emissions
articles.description: Revised figures increase fears about energy-intensive datacentres worsening climate emergencyThe UK government vastly underestimated the climate impact of artificial intelligence, it has emerged, after officials raised their estimate of carbon emissions from AI by a factor of more than 100.According to new data quietly published this week, energy use by AI datacentres in the UK could cause the emission of up to 123m tonnes of carbon dioxide (CO₂) – about as much as generated by 2.7 million peopl

summary.phenomenon: It has been revealed that the UK government significantly underestimated the climate impact of artificial intelligence (AI), as it raised its estimate of AI's climate impact by more than 100 times. According to new data published this week, energy use by AI data centers in the UK could emit up to 123 million tonnes of carbon dioxide (CO₂), equivalent to the amount emitted by approximately 2.7 million people.
summary.cause: Carbon emissions are generated due to the energy-intensive nature of AI data centers.
summary.outlook: Concerns are growing that energy-intensive data centers could worsen the climate emergency.

articles.original_description: Revised figures increase fears about energy-intensive datacentres worsening climate emergencyThe UK government vastly underestimated the climate impact of artificial intelligence, it has emerged, after officials raised their estimate of carbon emissions from AI by a factor of more than 100.According to new data quietly published this week, energy use by AI datacentres in the UK could cause the emission of up to 123m tonnes of carbon dioxide (CO₂) – about as much as generated by 2.7 million peopl

articles.title: Tokyo workers encouraged to wear shorts to cut energy costs and keep cool
articles.description: Officials hope more casual attire for public servants will save electricity during Iran war as summer heat approachesPublic servants working for the Tokyo metropolitan government are being encouraged to swap their suits for shorts this summer to combat sweltering heat and rising energy costs caused by the US-Israel war on Iran.Inspired by Japan’s Cool Biz energy-saving initiative, Tokyo officials hope the measure will cut dependence on air conditioning. Continue reading...

summary.phenomenon: Tokyo Metropolitan Government officials are being encouraged to wear shorts this summer to cope with the sweltering heat and rising energy costs.
summary.cause: The increase in energy costs is attributed to the US-Israel war on Iran, and this measure is inspired by Japan's Cool Biz energy-saving initiative.
summary.outlook: Officials expect this measure to reduce electricity consumption and lower dependence on air conditioning.

articles.title: How frustration at Cop stalemates inspires first global talks on phasing out fossil fuels
articles.description: ‘Coalition of the willing’ gathers in Colombia to try to bypass petrostate blockages of Cop summits and chart fresh pathThe world’s first Transitioning Away from Fossil Fuels conference, co-hosted by Colombia and the Netherlands, takes place in Santa Marta, Colombia, from 24 to 29 April. A “coalition of the willing” – including 54 countries and various subnational governments, civil society groups and academics – will try to chart a new path to powering the world with low-carbon energy. Continue

summary.phenomenon: The world's first Transitioning Away from Fossil Fuels conference, co-hosted by Colombia and the Netherlands, is being held in Santa Marta, Colombia, from April 24 to 29, with the participation of 54 countries and various organizations.
summary.cause: This conference is inspired by frustration over the deadlock at COP summits and aims to bypass blockages by petrostates at COP summits.
summary.outlook: Participants will seek a new path to power the world with low-carbon energy.

articles.description: Divers are installing waterproof speakers in the ocean to help pull a coral reef near Jamaica back from the brinkThe northern coast of Jamaica once served as the backdrop for scenes in the James Bond thriller No Time to Die. But today, beneath those same turquoise waves, a real-life mission is unfolding: the race to pull a dying coral reef back from the brink.However, the tools a team of divers are carrying to the seafloor are not what you would expect to find in a marine biologist’s kit. articles.title: Three disasters in three years: Brazil’s deadly floods show women are ‘the first to die’ when extreme weather hits
articles.description: The climate crisis is accelerating the frequency of devastating events across the world, displacing millions and disproportionately affecting womenThe water mark on Naira Santa Rita’s wall told the story before she could find the words for it. High and brown, like a scar, it was the line left by the floodwater on 15 February 2022 – the night Petrópolis drowned.Within minutes, the mountain city she called home became a war zone. From her window, she watched bodies float past in the streets below.

summary.phenomenon: On February 15, 2022, a devastating flood occurred in the mountain city of Petrópolis, Brazil, with bodies floating in the streets, marking one of three disasters in three years.
summary.cause: The climate crisis is accelerating the frequency of devastating events worldwide.
summary.outlook: Extreme weather events disproportionately affect women, leading to them being 'the first to die,' and the climate crisis is accelerating the frequency of such events.

articles.original_description: The climate crisis is accelerating the frequency of devastating events across the world, displacing millions and disproportionately affecting womenThe water mark on Naira Santa Rita’s wall told the story before she could find the words for it. High and brown, like a scar, it was the line left by the floodwater on 15 February 2022 – the night Petrópolis drowned.Within minutes, the mountain city she called home became a war zone. From her window, she watched bodies float past in the streets below.
